senses.definition: Without harm or damage; free from bewitchment or mischief; not causing injury.
examples: The skaithless voyage passed without incident.
We hoped for skaithless days after the storm.

senses.definition: In certain dialectal or historical contexts, acts of mischief or harm; causes of trouble.
examples: The town faced many skaiths during the raid.
They spoke of the skaiths that plagued the countryside.

senses.definition: Injurious or harmful; dangerous or troublesome.
examples: A skaithy look crossed his face.
The skaithy weather kept travelers indoors.

senses.definition: In some contexts, a unit or term related to chopping or scaling; also a term in Norse-inspired culture referring to a poet or saga singer.
examples: The skal recited verses by the fireside.
An old skal is said to have chronicled the saga.

senses.definition: A person who engages in political corruption or flamboyant, showy misbehavior; a rogue.
examples: The skalawag politician slipped away into the crowd.
Locals called the corrupt clerk a skalawag.

senses.definition: A poet in old Norse or Icelandic tradition; a composer or performer of skaldic poetry.
examples: The skald sang of heroic battles.
He studied the works of ancient skalds.

senses.definition: Relating to skalds or their poetry; characteristic of skaldic verse.
examples: Skaldic style uses intricate kennings.
They studied skaldic meters and alliteration.

senses.definition: plural of skald; Norse poets who composed and performed skaldic poetry.
examples: The skalds chronicled kings and battles.
Old Norse skalds were celebrated storytellers.

senses.definition: The status or role of a skald; the craft or body of skalds.
examples: The skaldship was highly respected in the court.
He aspired to join the ranks of legendary skaldship.

senses.definition: Plurals of skaldship; multiple instances of skald status or tradition.
examples: The exhibition highlighted several skaldships from different eras.
Scholarships supported new skaldships in university programs.

senses.definition: A small unit of value or a term used in coinage or barter contexts; historically used in some dialects.
examples: Coins were counted by the skalpund in the market.
He traded goods worth several skalapunds.

senses.definition: A glance or look; a glimpse.
examples: A quick skance revealed the secret.
She stole a shy skance at him.

senses.definition: In Hinduism, one of the six tangibly manifested forms (the Skanda), or a reference to Skanda (Kartikeya), the god of war.
examples: Skanda is worshipped as the god of war and valor.
The festival celebrated the legends of Skanda.

senses.definition: In Buddhist philosophy, the five or six aggregates that describe a being: form, feeling, perception, mental formations, and consciousness.
examples: Understanding the skandhas helps explain the sense of self.
The monk spoke on how skandhas interrelate with suffering.

senses.definition: A person who speaks with slangy or brash speech; a slang term for a loud or boisterous person; sometimes used as a pejorative.
examples: That skanger shouted across the street.
They warned the skangers away from the market.

senses.definition: Plural of skanger; multiple loud or brash individuals.
examples: The bus was full of skangers arguing loudly.
Skangers gathered at the corner shop.

senses.definition: A promiscuous or unattractive woman; can be insulting or reclaimed in certain contexts; also a type of music or dance style associated with queasy, rough rhythm.
examples: That club play a lot of skank rock.
He called her a skank, which she rejected.

senses.definition: To dance or move to a skanky or coarse rhythm; to act in a promiscuous or disreputable manner.
examples: They skank to the beat at the party.
Stop skanking around and focus on the task.

senses.definition: Past tense of skank; moved to a coarse rhythm or behaved in a disreputable manner.
examples: They skanked all night at the club.
He skanked badly, drawing disapproving stares.

senses.definition: A person who skanks; a brash or disreputable individual.
examples: The skanker caused trouble at the bar.
They avoided the skanker in the alley.

senses.definition: Plural of skanker; multiple brash or disreputable individuals.
examples: The street was crowded with skankers and dancers.
The market warned of a few skankers lurking nearby.

senses.definition: Comparative form of skank; more disreputable or promiscuous in behavior or style.
examples: That club has become skankier since last year.
The outfits were skankier than before.

senses.definition: Superlative form of skank; most disreputable or promiscuous in behavior or style.
examples: That club had the skankiest crowd in town.
The skankiest outfits were worn at the after-party.

senses.definition: Quality of being skanky; disreputable or dirty appearance or behavior.
examples: He disliked the skankiness of the bar.
The skankiness of the outfit shocked some onlookers.

senses.definition: Plural of skankiness; multiple instances of disreputable or dirty appearance or behavior.
examples: The film captured several moments of skankiness across scenes.
They criticized the skankinesses evident in the crowd.

senses.definition: Present participle or gerund of skank; dancing in a rough, rhythmic style often associated with certain music genres.
examples: People were skanking to the beat.
She spent the night skanking at the club.
